passages.quote: So we understand in Buddhism that there are five laws, the first one being karma. called the law of heat. That's really to do with what we would call physics and chemistry, just matter. So those are laws which are separate from personal responsibility. Now, what I think is really pointing to here is things like the tsunami. So in the East, if you were a victim of the tsunami, they would tend to think that was because in some past life, you must have drowned somebody or something worse. So it gets a bit far-fetched. So we have to be clear that the Earth matter has its own properties, and we happen to be born at this time.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing karma to clarify the five niyamas and distinguish personal karma from natural laws.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't clarify the five laws or address the common misunderstanding about natural disasters being personal karma. This provides important doctrinal precision.

passages.quote: So when somebody steps into that universe and does us harm, that isn't our personal karma. That's coming from their decision-making. Our personal karma is how we react to it.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the discussion of ethics and karma to clarify the locus of personal responsibility.
passages.rationale: This is a precise, memorable formulation about where personal karma actually lies that the base lacks.

passages.quote: And that observer really recognizes it as a place of untouchability. Once you're established there, nothing can harm you. Really sort of grasp that because that's very close to our liberation, yeah?
passages.integration_hint: Insert where the base mentions 'the observer' and entering into the world we're creating.
passages.rationale: The base mentions the observer briefly but this passage adds a vivid, practical description of its significance for liberation.

passages.quote: So really, it's just a case of keeping it as a possibility. I mean, in the fullness of the teaching, it gives you some idea of the grand moral value laws that go right through the cosmos, through all the different levels of being. There is this law of karma, which is basically saying, if you're coming from a wrong place, you're going to end up unhappy. If you're coming from a deluded place, you're going to keep finding yourself in an unhappy situation. And slowly it's going to come through to you that maybe you're the problem, right?
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on rebirth where the base discusses keeping an open mind about it.
passages.rationale: This expresses the pragmatic approach to rebirth more eloquently and connects it to the moral dimension of karma.

passages.quote: There is an unborn child. There is an undying. There's something that doesn't die. There is an unconditioned. And then he says, if it were not so, there would not be an escape from what is born and dies and is conditioned.
passages.integration_hint: Could complement the base's quotation about 'the not born, the not compounded' with this more accessible paraphrase.
passages.rationale: This is a simpler, more accessible rendering of the same teaching that could help readers grasp the point.

passages.quote: So we understand in Buddhism that there are five laws, the first one being the law of heat that's how it translates um but it's really to do with what we would call physics and chemistry just matter so those are laws which are separate from personal uh personal responsibility now what i'm really what i think is really pointing to here is things like the tsunami So in the East, if you were a victim of the tsunami, they would tend to think that was because in some past life you must have drowned somebody or something worse. So it gets a bit far-fetched. So we have to be clear that the Earth matter has its own properties, and we happen to be born at this time.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing karma and rebirth to clarify what is NOT personal karma, before the section on ethical karma.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't distinguish the five niyamas or clarify that natural disasters aren't personal karma - this prevents common misunderstandings about karmic retribution.

passages.quote: So when somebody steps into that universe and does us harm, that isn't our personal karma, that's coming from their decision-making. Our personal karma is how we react.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the discussion of ethics and karma to clarify the precise locus of personal responsibility.
passages.rationale: This is a concise, memorable formulation that clarifies a subtle but important point about karma that the base doesn't explicitly address.

passages.quote: And if after all it's just annihilation, we just disappear, well, there's no problem in that at all, is there? I mean, there's no suffering involved in that whatsoever. So it's best not to come to a decision about that. There's no need, for instance, to come to a conclusion.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the end where the base discusses the difficulty of accepting rebirth and says 'it doesn't matter.'
passages.rationale: This provides a practical, reassuring approach for skeptics that complements the base's brief mention that 'the Dharma works for us whether we believe it or not.'

passages.quote: There is an unborn, There is an undying, there's something that doesn't die. There is an unconditioned. And then he says, if it were not so, there would not be an escape from what is born and dies and is conditioned.
passages.integration_hint: Could reinforce the base's quotation about 'the not born, the not compounded' with this more accessible paraphrase.
passages.rationale: This is a clearer, more accessible rendering of the same teaching that appears in the base in more formal language.

passages.quote: in the West we see time as progress. God began something here and off it went and it's going to end up over there. And our idea of progress is that it's going somewhere. Actually, the whole point of the universe, the whole point of creation by God is that it comes to an end, and then there'll be the big judgment, and some go to heaven, but the point is, we have this idea of progress, right? [...] But just as an aside, you'll notice that even though our technology and our science is quite unbelievable, our moral behavior remains much the same as caveman. I mean, there's not much difference between what happened at the beginning of the dawn of human consciousness and what's happening now. And this is because of greed, hatred and delusion. now in the east and in fact before Christianity Judeo-Christian civilization it would have been the same for the Greeks and the Romans the idea was much more cyclical so time revolves and of course you can see that in a sense that every day the sun rises and the sun sets and you know there's this cyclic and when you get the idea of a cycle there's no beginning there's no end it just keeps going on and on and on so it's a very different idea about what time is you see
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated in the section discussing Western scientific vs religious worldviews, to contrast linear vs cyclical time and its implications for rebirth.
passages.rationale: The base discusses scientific and religious perspectives but doesn't explicitly contrast Western linear time with Eastern cyclical time. This passage provides that missing framework which is essential for understanding rebirth.

passages.quote: Now, what was horrific to the people in the Buddha's time was that there didn't seem to be an end to it. That this was what was in store. Every life, every time you ended a lifetime, depending on your beliefs, some people believed your next life would be determined by your good actions. by what you did or what you didn't do. Some people believed it was just fate. So next lifetime, you ended up as a pig. And that was it. And then the next lifetime, I'd be human again. And for some people, it made no sense at all. For others, there was some idea of progression. That one went on and then went into higher realms. There was, of course, people who were annihilationists. you know this is the Buddha's time where when your life was finished that was it it was complete others thought you went up to this very high realm and then that was it cut off so there's masses of ideas but the general thing was that was there a way out of this see so that's that's the Buddha's that's what he means by at its basic thing about dukkha about suffering was there a way out of this onward going
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on rebirth by providing historical context about the existential dread of endless samsara that motivated the Buddha's teaching.
passages.rationale: The base discusses rebirth but doesn't convey the emotional weight of what endless rebirth meant to people of the Buddha's time. This passage adds that crucial existential dimension.

passages.quote: So to become disenchanted with something, to become dispassionate, in other words, not to have these feelings about craving and all that sort of stuff around things. And then finally, it's enough to be liberated from them. And of course, that liberation really means this complete disidentification and dispossession, mental dispossession of everything that's in this world. and that's what we mean by renunciation now remember we're talking about a relationship we're not talking about the actual things we're not talking about not eating pleasant food anymore we're talking about not having this wrong relationship with pleasant food you've got to be careful easy and else you end up you know just dying of starvation because you think you've got to give up food no you don't have to give up any food
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section on ethics and relationship to clarify what renunciation actually means in practice.
passages.rationale: The base emphasizes ethics as relationship but doesn't explicitly clarify that renunciation is about relationship rather than abandoning things. This passage provides that practical clarification.

passages.quote: Supposing, monk, there was a great stone mountain, a yojana long, and a yojana wide, and a yojana high, without holes or crevices, one solid mass of rock, a yojana seven miles approximately, at the end of every hundred years a man would stroke it once with a piece of cloth that great stone mountain might by this effort be worn away and eliminated before the eon would still not have come to an end so long is an eon
passages.integration_hint: Could be added as a vivid simile when discussing the vast timescales of samsara and rebirth.
passages.rationale: The base discusses rebirth and karma but lacks memorable imagery. This classic Buddhist simile powerfully conveys the incomprehensible duration of samsara.

passages.quote: Suppose, monks, a dog tied up on a leash was bound to a strong post or pillar. It would just keep on running and revolving around that same post or pillar. So too the uninstructed worldling regards form as self, feeling as self, perception as self, volitional formations as self, and consciousness as self. And he just keeps running and revolving around form, and around feeling, and around perception, around volitional formations, and around consciousness.
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted in the section discussing the five khandhas and self-identification, near where the base discusses how we identify with the psychophysical organism.
passages.rationale: This vivid image of the dog on a leash provides a powerful metaphor for samsaric existence that the base lacks, directly connecting the abstract teaching about self-identification with the khandhas to a memorable concrete image.

passages.quote: So what is this pillar that we're tied to, you see? It's the self. That's the pillar, that's the false pillar that we've driven into our existence, thinking that, well, this is what I am. And therefore, we're always going to run around the demands of the self.
passages.integration_hint: Could follow the dog metaphor or be integrated into the discussion of how the self is manufactured through identification with the psychophysical organism.
passages.rationale: This provides a clear, direct explanation of the pillar metaphor that makes the teaching about self-delusion more accessible and memorable.

passages.quote: Now remember this birth, aging and death is the birth, aging and death of the self. The Buddha does not suffer from the birth, aging and death of self. He's liberated from that. His body dies and the Buddha within, remains, goes who knows where.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section discussing what happens to the Tathagata after death, or near the discussion of the Buddha nature and liberation.
passages.rationale: This clarifies an important doctrinal point about what 'birth, aging and death' actually refers to in Buddhist teaching, distinguishing between physical death and the constant arising of self-identity.

passages.quote: It doesn't happen when you're asleep. When you're asleep, there's no birth, aging, and death of a self. And when you wake up, you see, you say, oh, here I am again. You say that every morning, don't you? Ding-a-ling-a-ling, oh, I'm here. Here I am again, you see.
passages.integration_hint: Could be inserted where the base discusses how we manufacture identity with the psychophysical organism, as a relatable everyday example.
passages.rationale: This everyday example of waking up makes the abstract teaching about the continuous rebirth of self tangible and relatable to ordinary experience.

passages.quote: In Buddhism, the law of karma is understood within the framework of the Four Noble Truths, and without this law, the truth would not make sense.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added near the beginning when introducing the relationship between karma and Buddhist teaching.
passages.rationale: Provides a clear doctrinal framing that explicitly connects karma to the Four Noble Truths, which the base discusses but doesn't state as directly.

passages.quote: Strictly speaking, karma means what we actually do. The results of our actions is properly termed viparka. These two words then mean cause and effect, action and result, karma, viparka.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when first discussing karma to clarify the technical distinction between action and result.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't make this precise terminological distinction between karma (action) and vipaka (result), which is doctrinally important.

passages.quote: Volition, O disciples, is what I call karma. It is through will that a person does something in the form of thought, word or action.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing how karma operates, near the section on ethics and relationship.
passages.rationale: This direct Buddha quote identifies volition as the key agent in karma, which the base implies but doesn't state with this scriptural authority.

passages.quote: Even if a murderer gets away with it in the world, his mind won't. The Buddha said, According to the seed, so the fruit is reaped. There is no escaping these karmic results in Buddhism.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section discussing moral equilibrium and how karma resolves actions.
passages.rationale: Directly addresses the base's discussion about people 'getting away with murder' with a memorable formulation about internal consequences.

passages.quote: The first pool is the outside world setting up a chain of reactions that affects the me in the world. Since I am in relationship with the world as soon as I do something it affects it. These effects go on and on until they come back to the original doer... The second stone drops inward into the pool of the mind, setting up a chain reaction which affects the me in myself.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing how karma creates results both internally and externally.
passages.rationale: The 'two stones in two pools' metaphor vividly illustrates the dual internal/external effects of karma, complementing the base's more abstract discussion of karmic results.

passages.quote: I am the owner of my karma, heir of my karma, born of my karma, related to my karma. I abide supported by my karma. Whatever I do, be it wholesome or unwholesome, I shall be heir to the results of that action.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing the personal nature of karma and ethical responsibility, perhaps after explaining how karma relates to liberation.
passages.rationale: This Dhammapada verse provides a powerful, memorable doctrinal statement on karma ownership that the base text lacks, reinforcing the ethical teaching with scriptural authority.

passages.quote: The Buddha taught there were five general laws that govern the universe. Briefly, the arising and falling of all material things, nature itself, is governed by the caloric order, caloric here meaning energy. This law approximates to what we would call physics, chemistry and biology. Secondly, there is the law of germinal order, which is really part of the caloric order, but refers to the vegetable kingdom. Thirdly, there is the moral order, and this is the karma we're going to look at more deeply. Fourthly, there is the psychical order, which equates today with the whole area of psychology. And finally, the order of natural phenomena, both physical and mental, which points to a deeper level, the absolute truths of the universe, such as the inherent transitoriness of all things.
passages.integration_hint: Add early in the chapter when introducing karma to clarify that not everything is karmic result.
passages.rationale: The base text doesn't explain the five niyamas; this passage provides crucial doctrinal context showing karma is one of five laws, preventing the common misunderstanding that everything is karmic.

passages.quote: The results of an action are unthinkable, nor are they to be thought about. trying to work out the results would lead one to madness or frustration.
passages.integration_hint: Include when discussing why the Buddha doesn't explain karmic mechanisms in detail.
passages.rationale: This reference to the Achintaya Sutta directly supports the base text's point about unknowability while adding scriptural authority.

passages.quote: The last conscious moment of this life will be the first consciousness of the next, and will, according to Buddhist psychology, be the main mental disposition throughout the next life. If someone dies with hate on the mind, that hateful state of mind will constitute the subconscious flow of the next life. Conversely, if one dies in peace or in joy, that will be the next life's subconscious stream.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the rebirth section to explain the mechanism of death-proximate karma.
passages.rationale: The base text mentions rebirth but doesn't explain the death-moment consciousness mechanism; this passage fills that gap with clear practical teaching.

passages.quote: Here we are not crushed by unhappiness, nor is our intelligence dulled, nor are we lost in pleasure and happy states of mind. In fact, we experience them all. We experience the despair of hell and the joys of heaven. This mental wandering about can bring us great wisdom once we begin to see things with right understanding.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the six realms discussion to emphasize why human birth is precious for liberation.
passages.rationale: This eloquently expresses why the human realm is uniquely suited for liberation, complementing the base text's more basic description of the realms.

passages.quote: The law states, as far as he's concerned, because this happened in the past, this is happening now. Because this hasn't happened in the past, this doesn't happen now. Because this is happening in the present, that is also happening in the present. And because that isn't happening in the present, that does not also happen in the present.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added early in the chapter when introducing the foundational law underlying kamma.
passages.rationale: The base text lacks a clear explanation of idappaccayatā (the law of this and that), which is the fundamental Buddhist principle underlying kamma. This provides the doctrinal foundation.

passages.quote: So the law of karma is only one of five laws that the Buddha taught. [...] So the first one is known as the law of heat, utu, and it actually refers to what these days we would call physics, chemistry. [...] The second law is bija, and it translates as seed, and it equates to our genetics. [...] The third one is citta, which means mind. [...] The last one is dhamma, and that's all the laws, the spiritual laws.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing what kamma does and doesn't explain, to clarify that kamma is only one of five causal laws.
passages.rationale: The base discusses rebirth and kamma but doesn't explain the five niyamas. This clarifies that not everything that happens to us is personal kamma, which is a crucial doctrinal point.

passages.quote: There was a case here in Totnes, I was told, of a charity group that collected clothes. Clothes for the poor in Africa. A very compassionate, loving idea. So they collected all these clothes and they all went to Africa. And they put all the tailors out of work. So that wonderful compassionate act ended up with massive suffering for a lot of people. Just because you do something out of the goodness of your heart doesn't mean that everybody's going to be happy with it.
passages.integration_hint: Could illustrate the point about how we cannot control the results of our actions once they enter the world.
passages.rationale: This vivid, relatable example demonstrates the unpredictability of karmic consequences in a memorable way that the base text lacks.

passages.quote: As the Buddha says, it's quite clear. Chaitanya, aham chaitanya, kama vandami bhikkhu. I call your will kama. Now, the Buddha here is using karma to mean action. Action. And the will is what empowers something to produce an action. And there are three actions. Actions of thought, actions of speech, and actions of body.
passages.integration_hint: Add when explaining what kamma actually means in Buddhist teaching.
passages.rationale: The base text discusses kamma and rebirth but doesn't include this essential definition from the Buddha himself that kamma is intention/will, not just action.

passages.quote: So, having made this decision, there's an action which goes out in the world over which we've got very little control and which will come back to us in some way. [...] something else is happening on the inside so that action, that act of will is now producing a circumstance within my own mind [...] We have the outside world, over which I have little control, and the inside world over which I have total control.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on how kamma works, distinguishing internal from external effects.
passages.rationale: This distinction between external consequences (uncontrollable) and internal conditioning (controllable) offers a practical understanding of kamma that the base text doesn't articulate clearly.

passages.quote: The Hindu system would suggest a string of beads. So all the beads are different, but there's this string that goes through it, a continuity, a sort of self that continues, which is steady throughout the whole of these little apparitions. Whereas the Buddhist idea would be more a line of billiard balls that touch each other. So they're all quite distinct. All arising dependent on conditions. Not entirely the same, not entirely different. But always arising, always arising, one after the other.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing the difference between Buddhist rebirth and Hindu reincarnation, near the discussion of the soul being same or different from the body.
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid and memorable image (beads vs billiard balls) that clarifies the doctrinal distinction between Hindu reincarnation and Buddhist rebirth, which the base mentions but doesn't illustrate so concretely.

passages.quote: The bad thing about the whole rebirth, reincarnation theory, we can call it theory, is that of course for the self it becomes a comforting thought. So I won't die after all. I'll keep going. Even though I might have to go through this little gate and it's painful, I will arise. And to actually turn around and just face for a moment that in fact there is no re-arising of this person, which is true. This person will not re-arise. It's a bit of a, whoa! So to actually turn around and face the annihilation of this personality, is a bit of a challenge, a bit of a challenge.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the discussion of whether rebirth matters for practice, to address the psychological pitfall of using rebirth belief as ego-comfort.
passages.rationale: Offers an important cautionary perspective on how belief in rebirth can become a spiritual bypass, adding psychological depth the base lacks.

passages.quote: Fear, elation, what would you call that? Excitement, anger, depression, peace. Now, if there is a rebirth, you can see if you die in any of those states, that's the one that's going to carry you. And that's why in every religion they make this big effort when somebody's dying to get around them the images of their religion, which is going to make them peaceful, joyful, people giving them permission to go so the sadness disappears, singing hymns, all that sort of stuff, being with them in the last moments.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section on rebirth to explain how the death-moment mental state determines rebirth destination.
passages.rationale: Connects the Kübler-Ross stages of dying to Buddhist rebirth teaching in a practical, accessible way that the base doesn't address.

passages.quote: Our moral life is only to do with our internal, our interior life. Of course it affects others. I mean, if I'm into stealing, that's going to affect people. If I'm into good works, it's going to affect people. But morality, my ethical life, has a direct effect upon my inner life and eventually should clear it up, should move it towards a purification. But things that happen to me on the outside, of course, are coming from other sources.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing karma and moral equilibrium, to clarify that external circumstances aren't always karmic results.
passages.rationale: Provides an important doctrinal clarification about karma affecting inner life versus external events coming from multiple sources, addressing the seeming inequality question the base raises.

passages.quote: I've got this awful feeling that they're all here. They're all here around us. And just like the human realm is a jumble of hellish people, ghostly, these hungry ghosts, they're all around us here now.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the description of the six realms to reinforce the psychological interpretation already present in the base.
passages.rationale: Adds a vivid, personal expression of the interpenetration of realms that complements the base's discussion of realms as psychological states.

passages.quote: karma here means action more than karma with the R karma it's come into our languages as result you know like you deserve it it's your karma but technically karma means an act and what follows is known as viparka
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the beginning when introducing the topic of kamma/rebirth to clarify the technical meaning of the term.
passages.rationale: The base text discusses kamma but never explicitly clarifies the distinction between kamma (action) and vipaka (result), which is a fundamental doctrinal point that readers often misunderstand.

passages.quote: Nobody can cause us psychological suffering. Can you grasp that? Nobody can cause you that. Physical suffering yes, nobody causes a psychological suffering that's all an internal process and if it were not so How could I become fully liberated from suffering unless I get rid of you lot? I've got to get rid of all human beings and anything that disturbs me before I can become fully liberated It's the very fact that I'm creating my own suffering that means that I can uncreate it
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section discussing how we create our own world and the relationship between ethics and liberation.
passages.rationale: This passage powerfully articulates the core insight about self-created suffering with memorable rhetorical force that the base text approaches but doesn't express as directly.

passages.quote: Glenn Hoddle, who I think, well, he says he was misinterpreted, who said that, says that if you're born, you know, with a deformity, that must be because you did something horrible in the past life. And that disgusted so many people that he lost his job. And of course, that wouldn't be the Buddha's point of view.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the discussion of mechanical karma and the ship story to provide a contemporary example of misunderstanding karma.
passages.rationale: This modern example complements the traditional ship story already in the base, making the teaching about non-mechanical karma more relatable to contemporary readers.

passages.quote: because that happened this happens and because that didn't happen this doesn't happen and because this happens now this happens and because this doesn't happen now this doesn't happen [...] The Buddha's position is that there are both of these laws. There's both this causality coming from the past, and there is also this contingency, this immediate meeting of these different lines of karma
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section on the law of karma to explain the Buddha's nuanced view of causality.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't explain the dual nature of causality (linear and immediate) which is essential for understanding why karma isn't fatalistic.

passages.quote: what happens to us from the outside is not our personal karma It's not the karma which leads to liberation It's always what's happening inside [...] when the Buddha's talking about liberation and karma he's talking really about the world we're creating inside ourselves that's the real karma that's the real comeuppance
passages.integration_hint: Integrate into the discussion of ethics and the world we create, before the section on rebirth.
passages.rationale: This clarifies the distinction between outer circumstances and inner karma in a way that directly supports the base's emphasis on ethics and liberation.

dharma_talks/S12E04_Kamma_RevisitedTo_pl.title: Kamma Revisited..To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: The Buddha was quite clear that what we experience isn't, you know, isn't always our personal karma. So we have to be pretty clear what we mean by karma or else we're going to get into all sorts of sillinesses.
passages.integration_hint: Could introduce the section on karma to clarify common misconceptions before diving into the realms and rebirth discussion.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma and rebirth but doesn't explicitly address the common misunderstanding that everything that happens to us is our personal karma. This clarification is doctrinally important.

passages.quote: We talk about the five niyama, the five laws, and many of them approximate to what we understand. They are the physical laws, the laws of hereditary, the psychological laws, then the law of karma, and then spiritual laws. And so you can see there it's one of five that might happen to us.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing how karma operates, to show karma is only one of five causal laws affecting our experience.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma extensively but doesn't mention the five niyama framework, which provides crucial doctrinal context showing karma is not the only operative law.

passages.quote: the karma which is to do with liberation is how the person reacts when they're in that situation... it's how the person reacts it's always this tanha see... So you can see in a case like that that it's how the person reacts... The actual what happens to your body is just pain. So we learn that here on the posture. We sit in a position and there's pain in the knees. That's just pain. And what we discover is by taking this attitude of investigation, objectifying it, then there's no suffering.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the discussion of karma by distinguishing between what happens to us versus how we react to it.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma's relationship to ethics but doesn't make this practical distinction between external events and our karmic reaction to them as clearly.

passages.quote: So if you can imagine all these strands of past actions, the physical world, the biological world, the psychological world, personal decisions, all meeting every moment. Now, if it were just that, then you could see, well, where would there be any order? It would be quite chaotic. And it's the conjoining of these two that's a sort of paradox. That some things are ordained from the past and yet come into the present and meet things that they didn't expect. And therefore every moment has this creativeness about it.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the discussion of karma to explain how past conditioning and present creativity coexist.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't address the apparent paradox between determinism and free will. This passage elegantly resolves that tension.

passages.quote: every action produces a habit so that after a while you definitely know how to open doors... If you put all these habits together, the sankhara, then what you have is your personality. In Buddhism, that's your personality and character. It's a set of habits. That's all it is. And once that's set, as it were, your destiny is ahead of you
passages.integration_hint: Could illustrate the section on how karma creates habits and shapes our destiny through accumulated actions.
passages.rationale: The base mentions habits briefly but this passage provides a clearer explanation of how individual actions accumulate into personality and destiny through the sankhara.

dharma_talks/S16E11_The_Kamma_of_Liberat.title: The Kamma of Liberation.To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: It is the will that I call kamma. [...] and when he talks about kama he's talking about an act he's not talking about karma as we would use it today meaning you know your comeuppance he's talking about an act and remember once an act is done whether it's a thought in the mind or an act of speech or an act of the body an action it has an effect it's an energy which goes either in the mind it goes into the psyche itself or through speech and action out into the world
passages.integration_hint: Insert when the base discusses karma and ethics, to clarify the Buddha's precise definition of kamma as will/volition.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma in relation to rebirth but never gives the Buddha's core definition that kamma IS will/volition. This doctrinal clarification is essential.

passages.quote: Because that happened in the past, this happens now. And because that didn't happen, this doesn't happen. [...] So that's basically the way most people would think of karma in the sense of cause and effect. Something you did in the past and you get clobbered by it somewhere in the future. And obviously there is such a thing. But if you think about that then you tend to get into fate. And the problem with fate, of course, is that you lose your free will.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing the law of karma to distinguish Buddhist karma from fatalism.
passages.rationale: The base mentions karma but doesn't address the common misunderstanding of karma as fate. This passage clarifies that karma includes present-moment creative action, not just past determination.

passages.quote: things happen to us which are completely outside our control. [...] So now, what we begin to understand is that, you know, things happen to us, which have nothing to do with us. It's just other people's ignorance, other people's carelessness, other people's, sometimes, genuine mistake. [...] Things happen to us quite out of the blue.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the discussion of karmic retribution stories to balance the view that not everything is personal karma.
passages.rationale: The base mentions the ship story as karmic retribution but doesn't counter-balance it. This passage provides important nuance that not all suffering is personal karmic result.

passages.quote: internally when we do something unwholesome it creates an unwholesome habit within us which you know creates a turbulence and often finds its way out in speech in actions [...] internally though, we're creating this internal world. And that's exactly what we come across when we sit in meditation. This is our creation. Nobody's created the inner world for us. This has all been done by our act of will.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing how karma works and its relationship to meditation practice.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma and rebirth but doesn't connect karma directly to meditation experience. This passage makes the teaching practical and experiential.

passages.quote: the manner of our death is not necessarily a direct karmic consequence of our actions. [...] even if you're the most peaceful, loving person on this earth, somebody will hate you for it. All these people like Martin Luther King, Gandhi, they all get shot in the end.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the discussion of karmic retribution to illustrate that violent death doesn't indicate past wrongdoing.
passages.rationale: The base mentions the story of the aid worker killed by ISIS but this passage provides more powerful examples (MLK, Gandhi) and makes the doctrinal point more clearly.

dharma_talks/S16E12_The_Karma_Concerning.title: The Karma Concerning Liberation.To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: So, for instance, when this terrible flood happened in Pakistan, was that because in their past lives they drowned so many people? So, making that sort of very mechanical idea of karma is something the Buddha would not have agreed to. He said there are all sorts of reasons why things happen.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing the five laws of karma or when cautioning against simplistic karmic interpretations of suffering.
passages.rationale: Provides a concrete contemporary example that clarifies the Buddha's nuanced view of karma against mechanical interpretations, which the base text lacks.

passages.quote: nobody can cause us psychological pain. You have to really sort of grasp that point. Nobody can cause us psychological pain. It's all being produced by us. Discomfort, yes, I mean, I hear the words, I hear that, but that's nothing. The internal stuff that comes up, the turmoil, the addictions, all that, are caused by me, inside.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the section discussing how we create suffering through our reactions and attitudes.
passages.rationale: Offers a punchy, memorable formulation of personal responsibility in karma that complements the base's discussion of how we manufacture our world and suffering.

passages.quote: if I look at all my happinesses they're always dependent on something over which I can't control. The worst is other human beings. You cannot trust another human being. They're generally horrible. And worse than that, they die. I mean, just when you've got this love relationship the person disappears.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing how seeking happiness through manipulation of the world leads to suffering.
passages.rationale: Adds a vivid, darkly humorous illustration of dependent happiness that makes the abstract teaching more memorable and relatable.

passages.quote: So we've got this edge, this knife edge of existence. See, no past, no future, this knife edge of existence. And when we can begin to recognize that, we get what's known as the wisdom of insecurity. Which means that you never presume. You never live on a presumption. You never live on an expectation.
passages.integration_hint: Could enrich the discussion of impermanence and the unknowability of the future in relation to karma.
passages.rationale: Introduces the evocative concept of 'wisdom of insecurity' which elegantly connects impermanence to practical liberation, a dimension the base doesn't explicitly develop.

passages.quote: when something happens like this financial crisis if it only stops at the point of economic pain, it is pain, then there's this liberation inside, there's this freedom inside to work with the situation. He doesn't mean to say that we don't go and hang some of these bangers. There's got to be justice somewhere. What I'm saying is that the internal horror is not necessary here.
passages.integration_hint: Add as a practical example when discussing how ethics and internal reactions relate to external circumstances.
passages.rationale: Provides a concrete, contemporary example showing how understanding karma liberates one to respond skillfully rather than reactively, bridging theory and practice.

dharma_talks/S17E12_Q_A_Rebirth_Identifi.title: Q & A Rebirth Identification Emotions etc..To play the media you will need to either update your browser to a recent version or update yourFlash plugin

passages.quote: But this teaching about rebirthing, we should be able to experience it now. Everything of the Buddha is known as Dita down there. It's something to be seen here and now. It's not true here and now, it won't be true in the future. That's all. It's as simple as that.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the discussion of rebirth to establish the principle that rebirth should be verifiable in present experience.
passages.rationale: The base discusses rebirth theoretically but lacks this crucial doctrinal point that Buddhist teachings must be experienceable here and now.

passages.quote: So let's just take the body. Is it reincarnating? or is it reverting? Reincarnation, the word suggests, is the same body arising moment after moment. Now, I think all of you know that every cell, every atom in the body is replaced every seven years. That's what they tell us. So tomorrow, when you look in the mirror, just remind yourself, this face wasn't here seven years ago.
passages.integration_hint: Add after introducing rebirth concepts to provide a concrete, accessible illustration of the distinction between reincarnation and rebirth.
passages.rationale: This vivid example makes the abstract concept of rebirth tangible and relatable through everyday bodily experience.

passages.quote: Now, according to the understanding, we're developing conditionings, these conditionings. So, if there is no rebirth after death, if we just annihilate, fine, but at least we've had a fairly decent life, I mean, we've been gentle, yeah? If we happen to rebirth and find ourselves in another place, another time, then these conditionings come with us. Because you don't arrive there with nothing. So these conditions come with us and then we'll be very happy that during the last life we did a bit of practice. So it's a win-win situation.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the end of the rebirth discussion where Bhikkhu Bodhi is quoted, as a practical argument for practice regardless of belief.
passages.rationale: This 'win-win' argument elegantly addresses skeptics and provides a pragmatic reason to practice ethics regardless of one's position on rebirth.

passages.quote: If I think I am this body, and it goes off, and it's going to die, then heck, I'm going to be afraid, aren't I? It's me. It's me that dies. So that identity has, at its deepest root, a real basic fear. And our problem is that we know we're not real, but we don't accept it. We know we're not real. You can walk out of here and that'd be the end of that. You might get knocked over by a car. Boom, boom, that's it.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the discussion of self and identity to connect the teaching on anatta directly to fear of death and rebirth.
passages.rationale: This passage powerfully links the abstract teaching on non-self to the visceral fear of death, enriching the base's discussion of identity and kamma.

passages.quote: You could say that the practice of vipassana is a preparation for a peaceful death. That's what you could say. one of these phrases you have to die before you die spiritually speaking that means die to this self before you die
passages.integration_hint: Add near the conclusion to connect meditation practice directly to the themes of kamma, rebirth, and liberation.
passages.rationale: This succinct statement ties together practice, death, and liberation in a memorable way that the base lacks.

dharma_talks/S26E03_I_own_my_actions_I_i.title: I own my actions. I inherit the results of my actions. I am born of my actions. I form relations through my actions. I live dependent on my actions. Whatever I do for good or for ill, I shall inherit the results.VideoKarma and Rebirth

passages.quote: So when we're self-criticizing, when we're full of aversive self-blaming, when we're beating ourselves up, it's all completely unnecessary. You do something unwholesome. It comes back to you. You bear the consequences. You learn from it. You make an intention not to do that again. And that's the end of it.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated when discussing how karma works without need for punishment or external judge.
passages.rationale: The base mentions karma functions autonomously without punishment but doesn't address the psychological liberation from self-blame. This passage offers practical emotional clarity about accepting karmic results without adding unnecessary suffering.

passages.quote: Whatever the body you end up with is not the direct consequence of your past actions, but the way you relate to it is.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing how karma relates to rebirth and physical circumstances, perhaps near the Hoddle example or when discussing genetic/physical conditions.
passages.rationale: This is a crucial doctrinal clarification that corrects a common misunderstanding about karma and physical disabilities/conditions that the base doesn't address directly.

passages.quote: And here we have, let's talk about a certain paradox, which is not understood in popular Buddhism. And that is that you can purposefully suffer and yet be inwardly perfectly happy. I once asked somebody in Sri Lanka, a born Buddhist, brought up as a Buddhist, why he thought Jesus Christ suffered such a terrible death. And he said, well, you know, he must have done something terrible in his past life.
passages.integration_hint: Could follow the discussion of karmic retribution stories from the Dharmapada to show a common misunderstanding of karma.
passages.rationale: This anecdote illustrates a widespread misunderstanding of karma and introduces the important distinction between voluntary suffering for a cause versus karmic retribution.

passages.quote: So remember that once you create a set of actions, you then develop a habit. And when we collect all our habits, that's our personality, that's our character, and it will drive us to our destiny.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on how unwholesome habits create unwholesome results, near the discussion of how delusion leads to acquisitiveness and aversion.
passages.rationale: This offers a clear, memorable formulation of how individual actions aggregate into character and destiny, making the karmic process more tangible.

passages.quote: If you die, for instance, with a lot of guilt on your mind, you did something which has just come up as you die and you die with that on your heart, in the next life, you want to do something about that. If your last thoughts are what a wonderful life you've had, what a wonderful relationship you had, then you'll be seeking the same thing in the next life.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the rebirth section where the base discusses how the karmic process drives rebirth into different realms.
passages.rationale: The base discusses rebirth conceptually but lacks this specific teaching about how the dying consciousness influences the next life, which is doctrinally significant.

passages.quote: All experience, all actions are preceded by the mind, led by the mind, made by the mind. Speak or act with a corrupted mind and suffering follows as the wagon wheel follows the hoof of an ox. And the opposite, all experience is preceded by mind, led by mind, made by mind. Speak or act with a peaceful mind or a wholesome mind and happiness follows like a never departing shadow.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated in the section discussing how karma works through ethics and volition, as a memorable verse summarizing the mental basis of karmic action.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma and ethics extensively but lacks this classic Dhammapada formulation that elegantly captures how mental intention precedes all karmic action - directly relevant to understanding how karma operates.

passages.quote: of all these threes, it is the mental action which is the most greatly blameworthy for the making of evil karma
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the base's discussion of karma by adding the Buddha's explicit teaching on the primacy of mental action over bodily and verbal action.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't explicitly address the hierarchy of body, speech, and mind in creating karma - this passage provides doctrinal clarity on mental intention being primary.

passages.quote: What is unintended, Lord, the Nigantha Nataputta does not describe as greatly blameworthy. But if he intends it, asks the Buddha, greatly blameworthy, Lord. And under what does the Nīgānta-nātaputta classify intention? Under the mental rod.
passages.integration_hint: Could be used as an illustrative dialogue showing how even the Jains acknowledged intention (mental action) as the key factor in karmic culpability.
passages.rationale: This exchange provides a concrete example of how the Buddha demonstrated that intention is the crucial factor in karma - supporting the base's ethical framework with a memorable debate.

passages.quote: he proclaimed to him the distinctive teaching of the awakened ones, suffering, the cause of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the path leading to the cessation of suffering. And right there, there arose in Upali, the householder, in that very seat, the dustless, stainless Dharma eye, whatever is subject to arising is subject to disappearing.
passages.integration_hint: Could enrich the section on liberation and stream-entry, showing how understanding impermanence leads to the first stage of awakening.
passages.rationale: The base discusses liberation and the path but this passage provides a vivid example of how understanding the Four Noble Truths and impermanence leads to stream-entry - connecting karma, ethics, and awakening.

dharma_talks/S31E04_Karma-_Specific_Cond.title: Karma- Specific, Conditionality the Five Laws, ethical behaviour, social karma-Neoliberalism.

passages.quote: When this is, that is. From the arising of this, comes the arising of that when this isn't that isn't from the stopping of this comes the stopping of that... when you put these two together you get a cause coming from the past into the present moment where it meets other causes coming from the past into this present moment. So it's much more clearer to talk about how things happen as a process of contingency. Every moment, lines of decisions, not only that, nature, whatever's happening in the outside world, the birds, constantly coming to meet into this present moment.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing the law of karma and how actions produce results, before the section on rebirth.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma and rebirth but lacks this clear explanation of specific conditionality and contingency, which provides the doctrinal foundation for understanding how karma actually operates.

passages.quote: The Buddha talks about five different laws. and the first one is called the law of heat and it refers to just basic chemistry and physics... The next one is to do with seed, that's what it's called, so it's genetics... The third one is the citta, the mind. And this is our psychology... The fourth one is karma... which is dharma. And that's all the teachings of the Buddha.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing what karma is and isn't, to clarify that not everything that happens is personal karma.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't distinguish it from other causal laws. This five-fold classification prevents the common misunderstanding that all suffering is karmic retribution.

passages.quote: If a child is abused, the reaction is the child's reaction. It's not the reaction, it's not caused directly remember by the person who's abused the child. The abuse is physical, it may be emotional, but the suffering comes because of that reaction. There, the trauma is an inward process... It's only because my suffering is caused by me that I can be rid of it. That doesn't, when we think about that, doesn't excuse the abuser.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section discussing how we create suffering for ourselves through our reactions.
passages.rationale: This provides a powerful, nuanced example of how karma works internally while maintaining ethical accountability, addressing a common misunderstanding about Buddhist teachings on suffering.

passages.quote: There may or may not be rebirth, but it might be wise to act as if there is rebirth.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the end where the base discusses whether belief in rebirth matters for practice.
passages.rationale: This Pascal's wager-style formulation offers a pragmatic approach to rebirth that complements the base's statement that 'the Dharma works for us whether we believe it or not.'

passages.quote: So an intention comes, we've seen that clearly, we empower it, and it produces an action. When you repeat similar actions, then you get a habit. And when you put all your habits together, that's your personality. Once you've got a personality and character, then you're moving towards your destiny.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing how karma shapes our experience, near the section on habits and unwholesome results.
passages.rationale: This provides a clear, memorable progression from intention to destiny that the base lacks, making the karmic process more concrete and understandable.

dharma_talks/S31E12_QA_Kamma_FreeWill_Me.title: Q&A Kamma / FreeWill , Metta/attachment, Momentary Concentration (khanika samadhi)

passages.quote: karma here meaning our conditioning our own personal conditioning and this this conditioning is always within conditions it's never we're never completely separate from the world around us or the relationships around us or so it's always a sort of negotiated process where we're working in a situation from a particular intention and you haven't a clue what's going to happen once you activate it the general rule of course is that if you do something wholesome something wholesome arises out of it and if you do something unwholesome unwholesomeness arises
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated early in the base when introducing karma, before discussing rebirth
passages.rationale: Provides a clear, accessible definition of karma as personal conditioning within conditions, which the base lacks - it jumps to rebirth without defining karma itself

passages.quote: if we want to be strictly correct, kamma is your action. The consequence is viparka. But I know these days we just use the word kamma to mean consequences. So these are the consequences that are in us, in our own hearts because of past action. And you can't get rid of them just like that. You have to allow them to express themselves.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing how karma works and its relationship to meditation practice
passages.rationale: Offers a precise doctrinal clarification distinguishing kamma from vipaka that the base doesn't address, plus practical insight about purification

passages.quote: if everything was just cause and effect from the past, where would creativity come from? It would be a sort of repetitive process [...] but on the other hand if there's no past if everything happens just spontaneous they're not have you have some sort of chaos so there's the creativity things come together from the past to create a present moment and that's the creative moment
passages.integration_hint: Could enrich the section on karma and free will, complementing the base's discussion of determinism vs. eternalism
passages.rationale: Addresses the philosophical tension between fate and free will in karma that the base doesn't explore, offering a middle-way perspective

passages.quote: when you see somebody uh you know going down the biscuit aisle thinking that they have a choice you know as to what they're going to choose [...] you go all the way along 100 biscuits at tesco's and then and you keep coming back and you start centering on chocolate biscuits and in the end you think to hell with it i'll have my job
passages.integration_hint: Could illustrate the discussion of karma and conditioning with a relatable everyday example
passages.rationale: Provides a vivid, humorous everyday example of how conditioning operates that makes the abstract concept of karma tangible and accessible

passages.quote: these internal turbulences need not come out as memories. They need not come out as emotions. They just come out as pain. They can just come out as pain. Yeah. unfortunately. And when the pain comes out, you know something's gone because, you know, but you haven't a clue what it was. And the wonderful thing is you don't have to know.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added when discussing how karma manifests and is purified through practice
passages.rationale: Offers practical insight about how karmic purification actually works in meditation, which the base mentions abstractly but doesn't explain experientially

international_talks/S09E03_Le_Kamma_de_l.title: Le Kamma de la Liberation(Lausanne Oct 2010)

passages.quote: He said that there were two causes for something to appear now. [...] So something happens in the past which affects the future and we experience something that happened in the past. [...] Now, in the present moment, there is a meeting of many lines of karma. [...] But it's the junction between the two that the Buddha saw as being the reason why things happen. So there is an order in the universe but also that which cannot be known because you never know which lines of cause are going to meet in a given situation.
passages.integration_hint: In the section discussing karma and rebirth, before the discussion of the five laws.
passages.rationale: This provides a clearer doctrinal explanation of how karma operates through the meeting of causal lines in the present moment, which the base text lacks when discussing karma.

passages.quote: So the first law is the physical laws. Physics and chemistry. [...] And then there is the law of biology. [...] The third law is the law of the mind, psychology. [...] And there is also the law of karma and the law of karma is concerned with the wholesome and unwholesome actions that we do. [...] The fifth law is the spiritual laws. And they are the teaching of the Buddha of the four noble truths, and so on.
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated into the discussion of karma to show that karma is only one of five laws governing existence.
passages.rationale: The base text discusses karma but doesn't mention the five niyamas (laws), which is crucial for understanding that not everything that happens is due to karma - addressing a common misunderstanding.

passages.quote: For example, all the people who were drowned in the tsunami. It's difficult to imagine, I thought they were all drowned because in their past life, they had drowned cats.
passages.integration_hint: Could follow the ship story about the wife being thrown overboard to illustrate the absurdity of attributing everything to past karma.
passages.rationale: This vivid example effectively illustrates the problematic traditional interpretation of karma that the base text critiques with the ship story, but does so more accessibly.

passages.quote: When I was at Amarawati, there was a young man who was there. And he suffered from a handicap which made his whole body paralyzed and deformed. [...] I knew this young man and he was extremely at ease with this situation. And he had seen it as a spiritual path. [...] Although I never met the sister, I heard that she suffered a lot from it. She was very bitter. [...] Here we have a completely different reaction to a given situation and the world in which this young woman lives is created by her.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the section discussing how we create our own worlds and the relationship between karma and suffering.
passages.rationale: This personal anecdote powerfully illustrates how two people with identical physical conditions can create entirely different inner worlds based on their response, supporting the teaching that we create our own suffering.

passages.quote: So when you have a very strong sense of an observer, of someone who feels. And everything else is calm, quiet, still. So turn all your attention towards this sense of presence. It's the first object that we manufacture. It's the first object that we think we are. And that's where the self begins its constructions. And then the world.
passages.integration_hint: Could enhance the section where the base discusses 'the observer' and how 'that's where the initial problem lies because it's through the observer that you enter into the world that we're creating.'
passages.rationale: This provides practical meditation instruction on working with the observer that the base text mentions but doesn't fully develop.

passages.quote: Existuje pět zákonů vesmíru. První je stejný, jako je naše fyzika, chemie a tak. Jinými slovy materiální svět. Je známe jako uttu, což je v podstatě fire element... Další zákon je zákonem něčeho, co bychom nazvali genetikou. Slovo, které je zde použito, je bíža. To znamená síd. Znamená to semínko... A další věc na světě je Čitta. Dalším zákonem smíru je čita. A my bychom to přeložili jako naše psychologie... Tak a teď konečně přicházíme k zákonu karmy. A toto má obzvláště co dočínění s naším etickým morálním životem... A konečně je tu pátý zákon, který je duchovním zákonem, zákonem dhamy.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing karma to show it is only one of five cosmic laws (niyamas), not the sole explanation for everything.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma but doesn't mention the five niyamas framework, which clarifies that karma is specifically ethical causation, distinct from physical, genetic, psychological, and spiritual laws.

passages.quote: Když vás to přimáčkne při zemětřesení, znamená to, že jste v nějakém minulém životě stoupli úmyslně na tisíc mravenců? Kdyby jste plavili v moře, představte si, že plavíte v moře a teď vás strhne prout a utopíte se. Znamená to, že jste v minulém životě utopili vaše ština?
passages.integration_hint: Use after the base's discussion of karmic retribution stories to illustrate the absurdity of attributing all misfortune to past-life karma.
passages.rationale: Provides vivid rhetorical questions that challenge simplistic karmic explanations, complementing the base's critique of the ship story.

passages.quote: Gandhi byl zastřelen. Martin Luther King byl zastřelen. Ježíš byl křižován. Takže nikdy nevíte, vidíte? Nikdy nevíte.
passages.integration_hint: Add near the base's discussion of good people suffering bad outcomes to reinforce that ethical action doesn't guarantee worldly protection.
passages.rationale: Memorable historical examples that powerfully illustrate the point that doing good doesn't mean good comes back to you in worldly terms.

passages.quote: Takže jít do vězení není z budhova pohledu karmou. Je to mentální stav, který vystává, když jste chycení. Bolest ze ztráty osobní svobody... karma, o které mluví buddhisté, takže vzpomněte si, je to karma osvobození, je vždy výborný. Je vždy uvnitř.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the karma section to clarify that Buddhist karma concerns internal mental states, not external circumstances.
passages.rationale: Provides crucial doctrinal clarification that karma is about internal experience rather than external events, which the base doesn't explicitly state.

passages.quote: Když jsem byl na Sříglance, zeptal jsem se jednoho Sříglančana, který byl buddhistou. Říkal jsem mu, proč si myslíš... Zeptal jsem se ho, proč si myslí, že byl Ježíš Kristus ukřižován. On řekl, on musel provést něco strašného ve své minulém životě. Něco hrosného. Takže jestli chcete vědět o buddhismu, nezapomeňte buddhistům.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the ship story to provide another example of folk Buddhist misunderstanding of karma, with the humorous punchline.
passages.rationale: A memorable anecdote with a witty conclusion that reinforces the distinction between authentic Buddhist teaching and popular misconceptions.

passages.quote: The second thing that we can say is that the Buddha did not say that things happen according to our personal karma. He spoke of five causes for things happening. [...] The first is Uttuniyama. It's the law of heat. And it's how the ancients thought about the physical world. So everything began with heat and everything else appears out of heat. [...] So if you happen to be walking around Monte Santo and an earthquake comes and swallows you up into the earth, it's not because in your past life you stepped on a hundred thousand ants. It's because it was the time for the mountain to create this earthquake.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing karma to clarify that not everything is personal karmic result - the Buddha taught five niyamas.
passages.rationale: The base text discusses karma but doesn't mention the five niyamas which is a crucial doctrinal clarification preventing fatalistic misunderstanding of karma.

passages.quote: The second law, Niyama, is Bija Niyama. Bija means seed. And here the seed refers to what we would call genetics. Here we have the case, for example, of someone who is born with a genetic disease. And it would be very wrong to think that that person deserves that disease because in the past life they acted in such a way as to cause that disease. Because as we know, just from our own science, our genetic disease is a compound of hundreds of deaths and deaths that have nothing to do with us.
passages.integration_hint: Add after discussing the five niyamas to illustrate how genetics (Bija Niyama) differs from personal karma.
passages.rationale: Provides important ethical clarification that genetic conditions should not be blamed on past-life karma, which the base text lacks.

passages.quote: So just because you do something good in the world doesn't mean that something good will come back to you. There is no human justice out there. Because when you throw something into the world you don't know what conditions it will create. [...] So for example there was a charity in a small town in the south of England and they thought of sending clothes to Africa. [...] Unfortunately this ruined the clothing trade in that place. And so the whole economy began to go into crisis. So although the thought was very compassionate, not knowing the conditions of that country, this choice was not very wise.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the section on karma and ethics to illustrate the complexity of karmic results and the importance of wisdom alongside good intention.
passages.rationale: Provides a concrete, memorable example showing that good intentions don't guarantee good outcomes, enriching the base's discussion of karma with practical illustration.

passages.quote: So even if we don't want to accept this quality of rebirth in future lives, we must however recognise what is happening here and now. Take the body for example. The body we were born with is not the same one we find ourselves in now. They tell us that every seven years every atom in our body is different, it changes. [...] There is this continuous rebirth within the body, cells dying, cells being born, a continuous change.
passages.integration_hint: Add to the rebirth discussion to show how rebirth can be understood experientially in the present moment, not just as post-mortem doctrine.
passages.rationale: Offers a pragmatic, accessible way to understand rebirth that complements the base's more cosmological treatment and may help skeptical readers.

passages.quote: When this awareness comes out and is separate from the physical and mental body, this is a spiritual experience and it happens to people even during meditation.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing near-death experiences and the separation of consciousness from the body.
passages.rationale: Extends the base's brief mention of near-death experiences by noting this can also occur in meditation, connecting rebirth doctrine to meditative experience.

youtube_channel/S01E115_Upali_is_convert.title: Upali is converted by the Buddha MN56

passages.quote: All experience, all actions are preceded by the mind, led by the mind, made by the mind. Speak or act with a corrupted mind and suffering follows as the wagon wheel follows the hoof of an ox. And the opposite all experience is preceded by mind led by mind made by mind speak or act with a peaceful mind or a wholesome mind and happiness follows like a never departing shadow
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated in the section discussing how karma works through volitional action, to emphasize the primacy of mind in karmic formation.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma and rebirth but lacks this foundational Dhammapada verse that elegantly summarizes how mental intention precedes all karmic action - a key doctrinal point for understanding how karma operates.

passages.quote: it's within the mind that we have this will this chaitanya and it's these decisions that we make that produce our actions in this case unwholesome actions it all begins in the mind so as soon as we're aware of something in the mind which is unwholesome and we we sort of allow it to arise and pass away then it cannot express itself through our words through our speech and through our actions
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section on ethics and how unwholesome habits are created through the three attitudes of acquisitiveness, aversion and fear.
passages.rationale: This passage provides a practical clarification of how mental intention (chaitanya/will) is the root of karmic action, complementing the base's discussion of how delusion leads to unwholesome actions and habits.

passages.quote: what is unintended lord the nigantha nataputta does not describe as greatly blameworthy But if he intends it, asks the Buddha, greatly brainworthy, Lord. And under what does the Nīgānta-Nātaputta classify intention? Under the mental rod, Lord.
passages.integration_hint: Could be used as a concrete example when discussing how karma is created through intention rather than mere physical action.
passages.rationale: This dialogue illustrates the Buddha's teaching that intention (mental action) is what makes karma blameworthy - a key doctrinal point that enriches the base's discussion of karmic law.

youtube_channel/S01E122_Reflections_and_.title: Reflections and Contemplative Practice of Kamma

passages.quote: beings are owners of their actions, heirs of their actions. They originate from their actions, are bound to their actions, have their actions as their refuge. it is action that distinguishes beings from inferior and superior so this is not the distinguishment according to artistic or intellectual or physical talent this is to do with morals it's to do with whether we are moral or immoral virtuous the equation is that the more deluded you are the more unethical you will be and the more wise you are the more ethical
passages.integration_hint: Could be integrated where the base discusses ethics and karma, providing a clear doctrinal statement on how action distinguishes beings.
passages.rationale: Provides a precise canonical formulation about karma and ethics that the base discusses but doesn't quote directly, strengthening the doctrinal foundation.

passages.quote: the consequences that come from that are dependent upon the intention. So remember, the role of intention in the Buddha's teachings is crucial, and to be separated from desire. So there may be a desire to say something angry, but you see it and you let it go but if you speak angrily then that will have consequences remember it goes into a matrix the inner matrix of our psychology making us more angry and the outer matrix which is all these people coming together to listen to what you said
passages.integration_hint: Could be added where the base discusses how actions create habits and unwholesome results, clarifying the inner/outer matrix of karmic consequences.
passages.rationale: Offers a clearer explanation of how intention works and the dual matrix (inner psychology, outer social) of karmic consequences, which the base touches on but doesn't articulate as clearly.

passages.quote: What is your karma, no matter how unhappy you are with the body you've received? And it might not be that it's disabled. It might be that it's just ugly. You don't like it. Whatever the body you end up with is not the direct consequence of your past actions, but the way you relate to it is.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added near the discussion of rebirth and karmic results to clarify a common misunderstanding about genetic conditions and karma.
passages.rationale: Provides an important doctrinal clarification that corrects a popular misunderstanding about karma and physical conditions, which the base doesn't address.

passages.quote: once you create a set of actions, you then develop a habit. And when we collect all our habits, that's our personality, that's our character, and it will drive us to our destiny. And our job is to see what is driving us to an unfortunate destiny, to perdition, and what is driving us towards a heavenly destiny.
passages.integration_hint: Could strengthen the section where the base discusses how habits are created through unwholesome actions and attitudes.
passages.rationale: Expresses more elegantly and completely the relationship between actions, habits, character, and destiny that the base discusses in a more fragmented way.

passages.quote: you can purposefully suffer and yet be inwardly perfectly happy. I once asked somebody in Sri Lanka, a born Buddhist, brought up as a Buddhist, why he thought Jesus Christ suffered such a terrible death. And he said, well, you know, he must have done something terrible in his past life. And that's the way, we might say, regular Buddhists, born Buddhists would think of that... And so there's very little understanding that you can put yourself out for a cause, you know. You can become a martyr to a cause.
passages.integration_hint: Could be added to the rebirth/karma section to illustrate a common misunderstanding and add nuance about intentional suffering for a cause.
passages.rationale: Provides a memorable anecdote that illustrates a common misunderstanding about karma while introducing the important distinction about voluntary suffering for noble purposes.

passages.quote: The first was that his heart and mind were clear of all defilements; the second, that he could review his countless rebirths and see they were driven by his ethical decisions; and third, was his ability to see beings moving from one plane of existence to another driven by the same law. What was at first a law that pertained to him alone became a universal law. And the law was inexorable.
passages.integration_hint: Insert when discussing the Buddha's awakening and the three knowledges, to provide a clearer articulation of how personal law became universal law.
passages.rationale: The base mentions the three knowledges briefly but this passage articulates the progression from personal to universal law more elegantly and adds the phrase 'inexorable' which emphasizes the certainty of kamma.

passages.quote: These Laws tell us that not everything that happens to us is our personal kamma. We don't 'deserve' everything that happens to us. When it comes to natural disasters, to be caught up in a tsunami or earthquake doesn't mean we deserved to have all our property destroyed or lose our lives. If a person is born with a hereditary disease, it doesn't mean they did something terrible in their past life.
passages.integration_hint: Add after the discussion of rebirth and realms to clarify a common misunderstanding about kamma that the base touches on with the ship story.
passages.rationale: The base mentions the problematic view that suffering equals past-life retribution (the ship story) but doesn't explicitly correct this misconception. This passage provides a clear doctrinal clarification.

passages.quote: A charity that collected clothes to send to a poor country unwittingly destroyed the local tailor trade which in turn had a knock-on effect. They had to stop sending clothes.
passages.integration_hint: Insert in the ethics section to illustrate how even well-intentioned actions can have unforeseen consequences in the world.
passages.rationale: The base discusses ethics extensively but lacks concrete modern examples of how karmic consequences in the external world are unpredictable. This vivid example fills that gap.

passages.quote: So the kamma that pertains to liberation is that which pertains to our interior life. In Pali, the language of the Theravada scriptures, the word, kamma, strictly refers to the intentional act, and the consequence is properly termed vipaka.
passages.integration_hint: Add when discussing the karmic process to provide the technical distinction between kamma and vipaka that the base lacks.
passages.rationale: The base discusses kamma but doesn't make the precise doctrinal distinction between the intentional act (kamma) and its result (vipaka), which is important for understanding the teaching.

passages.quote: This tells us we can change ourselves. The Buddha does not deny the self. He simply says it has no intrinsic reality. It is dependent on inner and outer circumstances. So we can begin to change the self away from one that is unhappy and lacking in self-esteem to one that is happy and high in self-esteem.
passages.integration_hint: Insert near the discussion of the self and identity to clarify the practical implications of anatta for self-transformation.
passages.rationale: The base discusses the self as a manufactured identity but doesn't explicitly state that we can change it or that Buddha doesn't deny the self outright. This passage offers a practical and nuanced clarification.

bhante_essays/S03E06_The_Second_Noble_Tr.title: The Second Noble Truth -- Kamma Vipaka

passages.quote: In this fathom long body, I declare is the world, the origin of the world, the cessation of the world and the path leading to the cessation of the world. This is another formulation of the Four Noble Truths, but from the point of view of Kamma.
passages.integration_hint: Could be placed near the base's ending quote about 'this fathom-long body' to explicitly connect it to the Four Noble Truths and kamma framework.
passages.rationale: The base ends with this quote but doesn't explicitly frame it as a kamma-based formulation of the Four Noble Truths, which this passage clarifies.

passages.quote: There is no concept of punishment in Buddhism. Everything that happens to us are consequences. Punishment, as such, is something human beings have produced for themselves! It's something human beings do to each other out of revenge or a sense of so-called righteousness.
passages.integration_hint: Insert where the base discusses karmic results and moral equilibrium, to clarify that kamma is about consequences not punishment.
passages.rationale: The base mentions 'you have to forget the idea of punishment' but doesn't elaborate; this passage provides a fuller doctrinal clarification.

passages.quote: When a person does something, two stones drop into two pools. The first pool is the outside world, setting up a chain of reactions that effects the 'me-in-the-world'. Since I am in relationship with the world, as soon as I do something, it effects it. These effects go on and on, until they come back to the original doer... The second stone drops inward into the pool of the mind, setting up a chain reaction which effects the 'me-in-myself'.
passages.integration_hint: Could enrich the section on how karma creates results both inwardly and outwardly, providing a vivid metaphor for the dual effect of actions.
passages.rationale: The base discusses how actions create habits and unwholesome results but lacks this elegant two-pools metaphor that illustrates internal and external karmic effects.

passages.quote: Volition, O Disciples, is what I call Kamma. It is through will that a person does something in the form of thought, word or action.
passages.integration_hint: Add where the base discusses the driving force of karma and ethics, to provide the Buddha's precise definition of kamma as volition.
passages.rationale: The base discusses karma extensively but never provides this foundational definition that volition/intention is the essence of kamma.

passages.quote: Even a wrong doer may still find happiness, So long as his unskilful behaviour does not bear fruit. But when his unskilful behaviour does bear fruit, He will meet with their unwholesome consequences. Even a good person may meet with suffering So long as his skilful behaviour does not bear fruit. But when that skilful behaviour does bear fruit, He will enjoy the benefits of that skilful behaviour.
passages.integration_hint: Insert where the base discusses how 'people get away with murder' and good people suffer, as scriptural support for delayed karmic fruition.
passages.rationale: The base raises the question of moral equilibrium and delayed results but lacks scriptural backing; these Dhammapada verses directly address this.
